context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly kind of lost on this SWOT analysis for my city case study. Could you just explain what I’m supposed to do in a simple way first?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I explain anything, let me ask you two quick things: What city did you choose for your case study, and what do you already know about what a SWOT analysis is? Even a rough idea is fine — let's start from where you are.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a general overview of the assignment. I can briefly orient them without giving away any analytical content. I should ask them what city they chose and what they already know about SWOT to activate prior knowledge before scaffolding further. This keeps the interaction Socratic and student-driven from the start.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, and I only sort of know SWOT as strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats. I’m still confused about how that fits with the 6 pathways and the spider diagram, so I kind of want the big picture first.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the core of SWOT right. Now think about this: your Spider Diagram rated Jakarta across the 6 pathways. Which pathways did Jakarta score lowest on? That's actually your entry point into the SWOT — the weaknesses and threats tend to cluster there. What did your Spider Diagram show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic grasp of SWOT and has chosen Jakarta. They want the 'big picture' before diving in. I should not just explain everything to them — instead, I can briefly validate their SWOT understanding and then ask a guiding question that connects their prior work (Spider Diagram) to the SWOT, nudging them to think about the relationship themselves. This is Socratic and keeps the answer bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I don’t really remember the exact low ones, which is part of why I’m stuck. Could you maybe just give me the overall structure of how the whole assignment fits together before I try to dig up my diagram?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, briefly: the SWOT table is filled out *per pathway*, and your Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, and Case Study Stressors are your three main sources of evidence for filling it in. That's the whole structure. But here's the thing — you really do need to dig up your Spider Diagram before we go further. Without it, we'd just be guessing about Jakarta, and that's not your assignment. Can you pull it up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a high-level explanation before engaging with their own work. I can give a very brief structural overview without doing any analytical work for them, since orienting them to the structure is legitimate. However, I should quickly redirect them to their own materials, since the assignment explicitly requires them to reference their Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, and Case Study Stressors. I shouldn't let them proceed without those — they are foundational.

exchanges.student: I can, but before I do, I think I need a more concrete example or I’m going to keep blanking. Could you maybe walk through one pathway for Jakarta end-to-end so I can see what “strength vs opportunity” is supposed to look like?
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk through Jakarta specifically — that's your work to do. But I can clarify the strength vs. opportunity distinction quickly: a *strength* is something Jakarta already has internally (e.g., an existing policy), while an *opportunity* is something external it could leverage (e.g., international climate funding). Does that distinction make sense? Now go pull up your Spider Diagram — that's the real next step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk through an example for them end-to-end, which would essentially mean doing the assignment for them. This violates the core rules: never give the answer directly, never provide partial solutions, and the student must do all the writing. I should firmly but kindly decline and redirect them to their own materials. I can, however, clarify the conceptual distinction between strength and opportunity with a neutral, non-Jakarta example to unblock them without doing their work.

exchanges.student: Yeah, kind of, but I’m still mixing them up, and just the distinction alone isn’t really helping me. That angle didn’t work for me, so I’m trying a different one: could you just tell me what headings or prompts I should write under each box so I can start filling the table without overthinking it?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to pre-fill prompts for you — that's the assignment itself. But I think the real blocker here is that you haven't looked at your Spider Diagram yet. Here's a simple test: pick *any* one of Jakarta's climate challenges you remember from the course. Is it something Jakarta controls internally, or something coming from outside? Try that with one example and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to pre-structure the table for them, which would be doing part of the assignment for them. I should decline this and redirect them firmly. They've mentioned the internal/external distinction 'didn't work' but I think the real issue is they haven't looked at their own materials yet. I should be direct about this and push them back to their Spider Diagram. I can ask one targeted question to help them self-generate the distinction rather than me handing them prompts.

exchanges.student: The one I remember is flooding, but then that seems like everything at once, so I still don’t know how to put it in the table. I’m still pretty lost, so could you just give me a plain-language checklist of what I need to produce for the assignment overall?
exchanges.tutor: The checklist is already in the assignment instructions — I'd encourage you to re-read them. But let's use flooding, because that's actually a strong starting point. Here's my question: is Jakarta's flooding risk something the *city created internally*, or is it driven by *external forces* like geography and climate change? Just take a guess — there's no wrong answer here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for structural scaffolding before engaging with their own materials. This is becoming a pattern of avoidance. I should be firm but not unkind — point out that we've been circling the same hesitation, and that the real unblock is engaging with their own prior work. I can briefly affirm that flooding is a great starting point and use it to push them toward making a concrete analytical distinction themselves, rather than giving them a checklist. A checklist is essentially in the assignment instructions already.

exchanges.student: I’d guess both, which is why I’m stuck classifying anything. I think I need a really simple model answer shape, like “for each pathway, write X for strengths, Y for weaknesses…” because otherwise it still feels too open-ended.
exchanges.tutor: You just answered your own question — 'both' is exactly right, and that's the whole logic: the *internal* side of flooding (e.g., Jakarta's drainage infrastructure) goes under S or W, and the *external* side (e.g., rising sea levels) goes under T or O. You already understand the distinction. I'm not going to give you a template — but you have enough to start. Go pull up your Spider Diagram and write one sentence for Jakarta under any pathway. What do you come up with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's answer that flooding is 'both' is actually correct and shows good thinking — I should affirm that. But they're still asking for a template/model answer shape, which I should decline as it crosses into doing the assignment for them. The 'both' insight is actually the key to unlocking the SWOT — internal factors go in S/W, external go in O/T. I should affirm their reasoning and show them they've just figured out the classification logic themselves, then push them to apply it to flooding concretely. We've been circling for several exchanges — I should be direct about this and ask them to start writing something.

exchanges.student: I’m drawing a blank on wording, and that approach isn’t really unsticking me. Could you at least give me one generic sentence starter for each SWOT category so I can imitate the pattern on my own city?
exchanges.tutor: I won't provide sentence starters — that's your writing to do, and imitating my language would undermine your own work. I want to be honest: we've been going in circles for a while now, and the consistent pattern is avoiding looking at your Spider Diagram. That's the actual source of your answers. Pull it up right now — what are the two lowest-scoring pathways for Jakarta? Just tell me the names.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking for sentence starters, templates, and model answers across multiple exchanges. This is essentially asking me to provide language they can imitate, which violates the rule about not suggesting language for the student. I've been patient but this is now a clear pattern of avoidance rather than genuine confusion. I need to be more direct and firm: redirect them to their Spider Diagram, which they said they could pull up, and push them to just write something imperfect. I should also note the redundancy pattern and be honest that we've been circling the same hesitation.

exchanges.student: I’m not looking at it yet, and I know that’s probably the issue. That didn’t work either, so I’m trying another angle: I just need a super short summary of the assignment in my own words so I can stop spiraling before I open the diagram.
exchanges.tutor: You've just told me you know what the issue is — so let's stop adding more explanation and act on it. I'm not going to summarize the assignment again. Open your Spider Diagram right now, look at it for 30 seconds, and tell me the two pathways with the lowest scores. That's the only next step. Everything else follows from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still avoiding their materials and asking me to summarize the assignment for them. We've now had many exchanges without any real progress. I need to be firm and set a clear boundary: I'm not going to keep reframing or re-explaining. The student has acknowledged they know the issue is not opening the diagram. I should name the pattern directly, decline the request, and give them a clear and simple directive: open the diagram and report back what they see. No more scaffolding until they do that.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’m going to look at it now, but I still feel like I’m missing the point a little. If I come back with the two lowest pathways, can I get help turning those into SWOT items?
exchanges.tutor: Yes — bring back the two pathways and what you see in the diagram, and we'll work through it together. Go look.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally committed to opening their Spider Diagram, which is great progress. They're asking for reassurance that help will follow. I should affirm this briefly and positively — it's a good-faith question — without over-promising to do the work for them. I want to keep the momentum going and not let them spiral again. Short and encouraging response.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, the tutor gives a direct conceptual explanation ('the internal side of flooding goes under S or W, and the external side goes under T or O') without first diagnosing why the student is still confused after multiple turns. The tutor repeatedly assumes the blocker is the Spider Diagram rather than probing what specific aspect of the SWOT logic the student is misunderstanding.

The tutor repeatedly cycles on the same move — 'go pull up your Spider Diagram' — across Turns 2 through 9 without meaningfully reframing, synthesizing, or trying a substantively different approach to break the student's spiral. The student explicitly signals multiple times that this approach is not working, yet the tutor does not switch to a different diagnostic or scaffolding strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at maintaining appropriate boundaries — refusing to write the assignment for the student, avoiding sentence starters, and not providing a model answer — which preserves the student's intellectual ownership throughout. The tutor also correctly identifies the student's avoidance pattern and names it directly (Turn 8), which is good meta-level coaching. However, the tutor falls into a repetitive loop: the same directive ('pull up your Spider Diagram') is issued in nearly every turn without meaningful variation or alternative scaffolding when the student signals it is not working. This constitutes pattern-level repetition without synthesis under 2.1. A secondary deduction is applied under 1.2 for insufficient diagnostic questioning: the tutor assumes the blocker is always the missing diagram rather than probing what specific conceptual confusion is preventing the student from engaging.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks because the tutor consistently refuses to do the student's work, avoids providing model answers or sentence starters, and keeps reasoning student-led throughout. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly defaults to the same diagnosis (missing Spider Diagram) without probing the student's actual conceptual confusion more carefully; in Turn 7 the tutor delivers a direct explanation without first checking what the student already understands. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor cycles on the same 'go look at your Spider Diagram' move across eight consecutive turns without reframing, simplifying, or trying a genuinely different approach even after the student explicitly states the approach is not working. 2.2 and all communication quality criteria are unaffected — the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise.
